title: Fried dough elephant ears
subtitle: Flat-fried yeast dough dusted with cinnamon sugar
excerpt: Elephant ears are an American fairground food: a piece of yeasted dough stretched paper-thin, deep-fried until it puffs and blisters, then dusted with cinnamon sugar while hot. The dough is the same as for a basic white bread; the technique of stretching and frying is what creates the distinctive thin, crisp-and-chewy texture.
sourceNotes: Elephant ears are a traditional North American fairground and carnival food, with roots in the Native American fry bread tradition and Eastern European fried dough pastries brought by immigrants in the nineteenth century. The cinnamon sugar coating is the standard American fairground dressing. This recipe is synthesised from the established North American fried dough tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Fry to order. The dough can be made up to 2 hours ahead and left to rest, covered.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: deep-fried at 180 degrees C, not oven-baked

glossaryTerms.definition: Elephant ears are stretched by hand and rolling pin to almost translucent thinness before frying. The thinner the dough, the crispier the result. Aim for about 3 to 4 mm thick, roughly the size of a dinner plate. The dough will spring back as you stretch it; let it rest for 2 minutes if it keeps retracting, then continue.

content.text: The dough for elephant ears is a simple yeasted white bread dough. The frying transforms it: a thin round of dough dropped into 180 degree C oil puffs and blisters within seconds.
thin is the only real skill. Dust with cinnamon sugar the moment each one comes out of the oil, while the fat on the surface is still hot enough to make the sugar stick.
Deep-frying: the oil must be maintained at 180 degrees C. Use a probe thermometer. Keep the pan on medium heat throughout and adjust as needed. Never leave hot oil unattended.

Mix the 300 g flour, 5 g yeast, 10 g sugar, 5 g salt and 2 tbsp vegetable oil in a bowl. Add 180 ml warm water and mix to a smooth, elastic dough. Knead for 5 minutes. Cover and leave to rest for 1 hour until soft and slightly puffed. Mix the 100 g caster sugar and 2 tsp ground cinnamon together in a shallow bowl.

Heat the oil to 180 degrees C in a wide, deep pan. Divide the dough into 8 pieces. Working one at a time, roll out on a lightly floured surface to about 3 to 4 mm thick and roughly the size of a dinner plate. Ease the dough into the hot oil, holding one edge and lowering the rest in gently. Fry for 1 to 2 minutes until the underside is deep brown with dark blisters, then flip and fry for 1 more minute. Remove with tongs, shake off excess oil and immediately press both sides into the cinnamon sugar. Serve at once.

items.problem: Dough keeps springing back when rolling
items.solution: The gluten needs to relax. Leave the rolled piece to rest for 2 minutes before continuing. Work on the next piece in rotation if making several.

items.problem: Elephant ear came out thick and doughy rather than crisp
items.solution: The dough was rolled too thickly, or the oil was not hot enough. Roll to a consistent 3 to 4 mm and verify the temperature reaches 180 degrees C before adding the dough.
